questionText: Let $n$ be an integer. Prove that '$n$ is odd' is a necessary and sufficient condition for '$n^2$ is odd'. Prove each direction carefully, using a contrapositive argument where it is convenient, and state your conclusion using $\Leftrightarrow$.

steps.description: Restate the goal as a biconditional.
steps.workingLatex: P: n \text{ odd}, \quad Q: n^2 \text{ odd}, \quad \text{prove } P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: 'Necessary and sufficient' means both $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $Q \Rightarrow P$, that is $P \Leftrightarrow Q$. So the proof has two halves, one for each arrow.

steps.description: Recall which arrow is 'sufficient' and which is 'necessary'.
steps.explanation: $P$ is sufficient for $Q$ if it guarantees $Q$; $P$ is necessary for $Q$ if $Q$ cannot occur without it. Establishing both gives the full equivalence.

steps.description: Start the sufficiency proof by writing n as odd.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q: \ n \text{ odd} \Rightarrow n = 2k + 1,\ k \in \mathbb{Z}
steps.explanation: We prove sufficiency first. Every odd integer can be written as $2k+1$, which turns the word 'odd' into algebra we can square.
Expanding the square lets us examine the parity of the result directly rather than guessing at it.

steps.description: Rewrite the square in the standard odd form.
steps.explanation: Factoring 2 out of the even part shows $n^2$ is 'twice an integer, plus one', which is exactly the definition of an odd number.

steps.description: Conclude that oddness of n is sufficient.
steps.workingLatex: n^2 = 2m + 1,\ m = 2k^2 + 2k \Rightarrow n^2 \text{ odd, so } P \Rightarrow Q
steps.explanation: The square comes out odd whenever $n$ is odd, so being odd is a sufficient condition for the square to be odd.

steps.description: Switch to the contrapositive for the reverse direction.
steps.workingLatex: Q \Rightarrow P \ \equiv \ \lnot P \Rightarrow \lnot Q: \ n \text{ even} \Rightarrow n^2 \text{ even}
steps.explanation: Proving $Q \Rightarrow P$ directly is awkward, but its contrapositive 'if $n$ is even then $n^2$ is even' is logically identical and much easier to handle.

steps.description: Assume n even and square it.
steps.explanation: An even integer is $2k$; squaring produces a number with an explicit factor of 2, so it is even.

steps.workingLatex: n^2 = 2(2k^2) \Rightarrow n^2 \text{ even, so } \lnot P \Rightarrow \lnot Q
steps.explanation: The square of an even number is even, which establishes the contrapositive and therefore the original implication $Q \Rightarrow P$.

steps.description: State necessity from the reverse implication.
steps.workingLatex: Q \Rightarrow P \text{ holds, so } n \text{ odd is necessary for } n^2 \text{ odd}
steps.explanation: Since $n^2$ can be odd only when $n$ is odd, oddness of $n$ is a necessary condition for oddness of $n^2$.

steps.description: Combine both directions into the biconditional.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{and}\ Q \Rightarrow P \ \Rightarrow\ P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: With both arrows proven, the two statements are equivalent, so '$n$ is odd' is both necessary and sufficient for '$n^2$ is odd'.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Both directions hold: $n$ odd $\Rightarrow n^2 = 2(2k^2 + 2k) + 1$ is odd (sufficient); and by contrapositive $n$ even $\Rightarrow n^2$ even, so $n^2$ odd $\Rightarrow n$ odd (necessary). Hence $n \text{ odd} \Leftrightarrow n^2 \text{ odd}$.
